intro: Leprosy in the biblical world (Hebrew tsara'ath; Greek lepra) was a broad diagnostic category covering numerous skin conditions — including modern leprosy (Hansen's disease), psoriasis, vitiligo, and other eruptive disorders — rather than a single disease. Leviticus 13–14 prescribes elaborate priestly procedures for diagnosing, isolating, and pronouncing clean or unclean those with such conditions, because ritual purity and social separation, not merely medical treatment, were at stake. Notable biblical lepers include Miriam (Num. 12:10), Naaman the Syrian general (2 Kgs. 5), Gehazi (2 Kgs. 5:27), and King Uzziah (2 Chr. 26:19–21). Jesus' willingness to touch lepers (Mark 1:41) and his healing of ten at once (Luke 17:11–19) signaled the arrival of the kingdom's restoring power and overturned the ritual barriers that enforced social exclusion.
